content:
We study global dynamics of the Poisson-Nernst-Planck (PNP) system for flows of two types of ions through a narrow tubular-like membrane channel. As the radius of the cross-section of the three-dimensional tubular-like membrane channel approaches zero, a one-dimensional limiting PNP system is derived. This one-dimensional limiting system differs from previous studied one-dimensional PNP systems in that it encodes the defining geometry of the three-dimensional membrane channel. To justify this limiting process, we show that the global attractors of the three-dimensional PNP systems are upper semi-continuous to that of the limiting PNP system. We then examine the dynamics of the one-dimensional limiting PNP system. For large Debye number, the steady-state of the one-dimensional limiting PNP system is completed analyzed using the geometric singular perturbation theory. For a special case, an entropy-type Lyapunov functional is constructed to show the global, asymptotic stability of the steady-state.

Key words. Poisson-Nernst-Planck system, global attractor, steady solution. MSC 2000. Primary 37L55. Secondary 35B40.

citation.title: Poisson-Nernst-Planck Systems for Narrow Tubular-like Membrane Channels

content:
1 Introduction
Poisson-Nernst-Planck (PNP) systems serve as basic electro-diffusion equations modeling, for example, ion flow through membrane channels, transport of holes and electrons in semiconductors (see, e.g., [1, 2, 21, 27] and the references therein). There are many excellent works on derivation of PNP systems from Boltzmann equations (see [1] and reference therein) by assuming the collision time is much smaller than the characteristic time. The PNP systems have been studied under various physical relevant boundary conditions such as the non-flux, homogeneous Dirichlet and Neumann boundary conditions. For those types of boundary conditions, in addition to the total charge conservation and the existence of various first integrals, Boltzmann H-functionals or entropy-like functionals are successfully constructed, which, together with the advances of Csisz¬īar-Kullback type or logarithmic Soblev inequalities, are applied to investigate the asymptotic behavior of the PNP systems and stability of steady-state or self-similar solutions (see, e.g., [3, 4, 8, 9, 10, 24, 28]).

citation.title: Poisson-Nernst-Planck Systems for Narrow Tubular-like Membrane Channels

content:
In the context of ion flow through membrane channels, it is physically unreasonable to impose the above mentioned boundary conditions on the whole boundary, particularly at the two "ends" of the channels. Instead, non-homogeneous Dirichlet conditions on the two "ends" are typically assumed. PNP systems supplemented with this type of boundary conditions result in quite different dynamical behavior. The total charges within the channels are not conserved and entropy-like functionals are not available in general, and, most significantly, the asymptotic behavior is different as showed in special cases in the present paper.

In this work, we start a systematic study of the PNP systems modeling ion flows through narrow tubular-like membrane channels in physiology. For definiteness, consider flow of two types of ions, S1 and S2, one with the positive valence őĪ1 > 0 and the other with the negative valence -őĪ2 < 0, passing through a membrane channel with length normalized from X = 0 to X = 1.

citation.title: Poisson-Nernst-Planck Systems for Narrow Tubular-like Membrane Channels

content: Denote the concentrations of S1 and S2 at location (X,Y,Z) and time t by c1(t,X,Y,Z) and c2(t,X,Y,Z). Then the electric potential ő¶(t,X,Y,Z) in the channel is governed by the Poisson equation őĒő¶ = -őĽ(őĪ1c1 - őĪ2c2), where the parameter őĽ is the Debye number related to the ratio of the Debye length to a characteristic length scale. The flux densities, J1 and J2, of the two ions contributed from the concentration gradients of the two ions and the electric field satisfy the Nernst-Planck equations D1(‚ąác1 + őĪ1c1‚ąáő¶) = -J1 and D2(‚ąác2 - őĪ2c2‚ąáő¶) = -J2, and the continuity equations ‚ąāc1/‚ąāt + ‚ąá¬∑J1 = 0, ‚ąāc2/‚ąāt + ‚ąá¬∑J2 = 0, where D1 and D2 are the diffusion constants of ions S1 and S2 relative to the membrane channel.

citation.title: Poisson-Nernst-Planck Systems for Narrow Tubular-like Membrane Channels

content:
The Poisson-Nernst-Planck system is thus given by:
őĒő¶ = -őĽ(őĪ1c1 - őĪ2c2),
‚ąāc1/‚ąāt = D1 ‚ąá¬∑(‚ąác1 + őĪ1c1‚ąáő¶),
‚ąāc2/‚ąāt = D2 ‚ąá¬∑(‚ąác2 - őĪ2c2‚ąáő¶).

PNP systems have been studied by many authors (see, e.g., [1, 2, 3, 4, 8, 9, 10, 15, 16, 17, 21, 24, 25, 27, 28]). Many works have been attributed to the one-dimensional PNP systems and particularly the steady-state problems (see, e.g., [15, 16, 25, 17]). Consideration of one-dimensional PNP systems is motivated naturally by the fact that membrane channels are narrow. To make this reduction more rigorous, we present a mathematical framework based on the ideas in [12, 13] and investigate mathematically the limiting process as the three-dimensional domain shrinks to a line segment.

citation.title: Poisson-Nernst-Planck Systems for Narrow Tubular-like Membrane Channels

content: More specifically, starting with the situation that ő©őĶ is a revolution domain about its length (őĶ is related to the maximal radius of the cross-sections of the channel), we will derive a one-dimensional limiting PNP system as őĶ ‚Üí 0. Differing from the simple one-dimensional version of the PNP system, this limiting PNP system encodes the defining geometry of the three-dimensional channel. As the first step in justifying the limiting process, we show the upper semi-continuity of the global attractors AőĶ of the three-dimensional systems at őĶ = 0. The existence of global attractors for the PNP systems can be found in [9]. It is expected that if the one-dimensional limiting system is structurally stable, then its dynamics determines that of three-dimensional system for őĶ > 0 small.

citation.title: Poisson-Nernst-Planck Systems for Narrow Tubular-like Membrane Channels

content:
We will thus examine, in this paper, the steady-state problem of the one-dimensional limiting system. For large Debye number, the steady-state problem can be viewed as a singularly perturbed one. We show that this problem can be completely analyzed using the geometric singular perturbation theory as in [23].

The rest of the paper is organized as follows. In Section 2, we give detailed formulation of our problem. The domain for the three-dimensional PNP system will be specified and, as the domain shrinks to a one-dimensional segment, a one-dimensional limiting PNP system is derived. We then state our results on the upper semi-continuity of attractors, on the singular boundary value problem of the steady-state PNP system. The proofs are provided in Sections 3 and 4.

citation.title: Poisson-Nernst-Planck Systems for Narrow Tubular-like Membrane Channels

content:
In Section 3, after some technical preparations, we show the upper semi-continuity of attractors. Section 4 is devoted to the geometric analysis of the singularly perturbed steady-state problem of the one-dimensional limiting PNP system. At the end of this section, a special case is studied for which a H-function is found and used to establish the asymptotic stability of the steady-state.

2 Formulation of the problem and the statements of main results
2.1 Three-dimensional PNP and a one-dimensional limit
We start with setting up our problem. The membrane channel considered here is special and will be viewed as a tubular-like domain ő©őĶ in R3 as follows: ő©őĶ = {(X,Y,Z) : 0 < X < 1, Y^2+Z^2 < g^2(X,őĶ)}, where g is a smooth (at least C^3) function satisfying g(X,0) = 0 and g0(X) = ‚ąāg/‚ąāőĶ (X,0) > 0 for X ‚ąą [0,1].

citation.title: Poisson-Nernst-Planck Systems for Narrow Tubular-like Membrane Channels

content:
The positive parameter őĶ measures the sizes of cross-sections of the membrane channel. For a technical reason (used in Lemma 3.1), we also assume that ‚ąāg/‚ąāX (0,őĶ) = ‚ąāg/‚ąāX (1,őĶ) = 0.
The boundary ‚ąāő©őĶ of ő©őĶ will be divided into three portions: LőĶ = {(X,Y,Z) ‚ąą ‚ąāő©őĶ : X = 0}, RőĶ = {(X,Y,Z) ‚ąą ‚ąāő©őĶ : X = 1}, MőĶ = {(X,Y,Z) ‚ąą ‚ąāő©őĶ : Y^2+Z^2 = g^2(X,őĶ)}. Thus, LőĶ and RőĶ are viewed as the two ends of the channel and MőĶ the wall of the channel. The boundary conditions considered in this paper are:
ő¶|LőĶ = ŌÜ0 > 0, ő¶|RőĶ = 0, ck|LőĶ = lk > 0, ck|RőĶ = rk > 0, ‚ąāő¶/‚ąān |MőĶ = ‚ąāck/‚ąān |MőĶ = 0, where ŌÜ0, lk and rk (k = 1,2) are constants, and n is the outward unit normal vector to MőĶ.

citation.title: Poisson-Nernst-Planck Systems for Narrow Tubular-like Membrane Channels

content:
Although the most natural boundary conditions on MőĶ would be the non-flux one (‚ąāc1/‚ąān + őĪ1c1 ‚ąāő¶/‚ąān)|MőĶ = (‚ąāc2/‚ąān - őĪ2c2 ‚ąāő¶/‚ąān)|MőĶ = 0, the above homogeneous Neumann conditions on MőĶ are reasonable (they are the consequences of the non-flux and zero-outward electric field conditions).

In this paper, we are interested in the limiting behavior of the PNP system when the three-dimensional tubular-like domain ő©őĶ collapses to a one-dimensional interval as őĶ ‚Üí 0. Naturally we expect a one-dimensional limiting system whose global dynamics is comparable with those of PNP systems for őĶ > 0 small. This important idea was applied by many researchers in studying the dynamics of equations defined on thin domains (see, e.g., [12, 13, 26, 29]). We follow the procedure in [13] to derive a one-dimensional limiting system but avoid expressing differential operators and transformations in local coordinates. As a result, the expected one-dimensional limiting system is more transparent.

citation.title: Poisson-Nernst-Planck Systems for Narrow Tubular-like Membrane Channels

content:
To derive the limiting PNP system, we transfer the őĶ-dependent domain ő©őĶ into a fixed domain ő© = [0,1] √ó D, where D is the unit disk, by applying the change of coordinates: x = X, y = Y/g(X,őĶ), z = Z/g(X,őĶ). In the sequel, we denote by L, R and M, respectively, the boundaries of ő© corresponding to LőĶ, RőĶ and MőĶ under the transformation. Let J denote the Jacobian matrix of the change of coordinates. Then, after applying Lemma 2.1 (a technical result for diffeomorphisms), system (1.1) can be rewritten in terms of (x,y,z) with boundary conditions. By inspecting the structural dependence of JJ^T on őĶ, we expect the one-dimensional limiting PNP system to be:
(1/g0^2) ‚ąā/‚ąāx (g0^2 ‚ąāő¶/‚ąāx) = -őĽ(őĪ1c1 - őĪ2c2),
‚ąāc1/‚ąāt = (D1/g0^2) ‚ąā/‚ąāx (g0^2 (‚ąāc1/‚ąāx + őĪ1c1 ‚ąāő¶/‚ąāx)),
‚ąāc2/‚ąāt = (D2/g0^2) ‚ąā/‚ąāx (g0^2 (‚ąāc2/‚ąāx - őĪ2c2 ‚ąāő¶/‚ąāx)), on x‚ąą(0,1) with boundary conditions ő¶(t,0)=ŌÜ0, ő¶(t,1)=0, ck(t,0)=lk, ck(t,1)=rk, where g0(x) is defined above.

citation.title: Poisson-Nernst-Planck Systems for Narrow Tubular-like Membrane Channels

content:
It was shown in [9] that, for any őĶ > 0, the three-dimensional PNP system has a global attractor AőĶ which is a compact subset and attracts all solutions with respect to the norm topology of H^1√óH^1. This result is based on an invariant principle discovered in [10, 11, 9, 28] for the van Roosbroeck models of semi-conductor. The PNP systems are basically the same as the van Roosbroeck models and we recall the invariant principle using the above setting.

Proposition 2.2. Let M be a positive constant with M ‚Č• max{őĪ1l1, őĪ1r1, őĪ2l2, őĪ2r2}, and let ő£ŐÉ be the subset of H^1(ő©őĶ)√óH^1(ő©őĶ) given by ő£ŐÉ = {(c1,c2)‚ąą H^1(ő©őĶ)√óH^1(ő©őĶ) : 0 ‚Č§ őĪ1c1 ‚Č§ M, 0 ‚Č§ őĪ2c2 ‚Č§ M}. Then ő£ŐÉ is positively invariant for the PNP system. More precisely, if the initial datum (c1(0),c2(0)) ‚ąą ő£ŐÉ and (c1,c2) is the solution, then (c1(t),c2(t)) ‚ąą ő£ŐÉ for all t ‚Č• 0.

citation.title: Poisson-Nernst-Planck Systems for Narrow Tubular-like Membrane Channels

content:
We remark that, for PNP systems with three or more types of ions, the above invariant principle is not available. It is not clear to us whether or not a similar principle still holds in this case. PNP systems with more than two types of ions are worth further studying.

The results in [10, 11, 9, 28] show also that the one-dimensional problem (2.6)-(2.7) has a positively invariant region ő£ŐÉ0 = {(c1,c2)‚ąą H^1(0,1)√óH^1(0,1) : 0 ‚Č§ őĪ1c1 ‚Č§ M, 0 ‚Č§ őĪ2c2 ‚Č§ M}, where M is the constant in Proposition 2.2, and problem (2.6)-(2.7) is globally well-posed in ő£ŐÉ0 and has a global attractor A0 in ő£ŐÉ0 ‚ą© H^1(0,1)√óH^1(0,1).

Our first result claims that the global attractors AőĶ of the three-dimensional PNP systems are upper semi-continuous to the global attractor A0 of the one-dimensional limiting system as őĶ ‚Üí 0, which partially justify the limiting process.

citation.title: Poisson-Nernst-Planck Systems for Narrow Tubular-like Membrane Channels

content:
Theorem 2.3. The global attractors AőĶ of the three-dimensional PNP systems are upper semi-continuous at őĶ = 0, that is, for any ő∑ > 0, there exists a positive number őĶ1 = őĶ1(ő∑) such that for all 0 < őĶ ‚Č§ őĶ1 and all w ‚ąą AőĶ, dist_{XőĶ}(w, A0) ‚Č§ ő∑, where XőĶ = { w : ‚ą•w‚ą•^2_{XőĶ} = ‚ą•w‚ą•^2_{L^2} + ‚ą•‚ąáw‚ą•^2_{L^2} + (1/őĶ^2)‚ą•wy‚ą•^2_{L^2} + (1/őĶ^2)‚ą•wz‚ą•^2_{L^2} < ‚ąě }.

2.2 Steady-state problem of the one-dimensional limiting PNP system
The steady-state of problem (2.6) and (2.7) can be rewritten as
őľ^2 d/dx (h(x) dŌÜ/dx) = -h(x)(őĪ1c1 - őĪ2c2),
dJ1/dx = 0, dJ2/dx = 0,
h(x) dc1/dx + őĪ1c1 h(x) dŌÜ/dx = -J1,
h(x) dc2/dx - őĪ2c2 h(x) dŌÜ/dx = -J2,
where őľ^2 = 1/őĽ, J1 = JŐĄ1/D1, J2 = JŐĄ2/D2 and h(x) = g0^2(x), and the boundary conditions are ŌÜ(0)=ŌÜ0, c1(0)=l1, c2(0)=l2, ŌÜ(1)=0, c1(1)=r1, c2(1)=r2.

citation.title: Poisson-Nernst-Planck Systems for Narrow Tubular-like Membrane Channels

content:
Since őĽ is large, we treat the problem as a singularly perturbed problem with őľ as the singular parameter. We recast it into a system of first order equations. Denote derivatives with respect to x by overdot and introduce ŌĄ = x, u = őľ h(ŌĄ) ŌÜŐá, v = -h(ŌĄ)(őĪ1c1 - őĪ2c2), w = őĪ1^2 c1 + őĪ2^2 c2. Then the slow system is obtained. Setting őľ = 0 gives the limiting slow system and the slow manifold Z0 = {u = v = 0}. The boundary layers are governed by the fast system after rescaling x = őľőĺ.

Theorem 2.4. Assume that őĪ1l1 ‚Č† őĪ2l2 and őĪ1r1 ‚Č† őĪ2r2 (otherwise, see Remark 4.1). For őľ > 0 small, the boundary value problem has a unique solution near a singular orbit. The singular orbit is the union of two fast orbits of the fast system representing the boundary layers and one slow orbit of a blow-up system for the regular layer. The limiting flux densities are explicitly given by formulas involving h^{-1}(x) integrated over [0,1], reflecting the geometry of the three-dimensional channel.

citation.title: Poisson-Nernst-Planck Systems for Narrow Tubular-like Membrane Channels

content:
Remark 2.1. The factor ‚ąę_0^1 h^{-1}(x)dx on the denominators in the expressions for the flux densities J1 and J2 reflects the effect of the geometry of the three-dimensional channel. For a cylindrical channel where the wall is defined by {Y^2+Z^2 = őĶ}, the corresponding integral factor is 1. The volume of the channel is ŌÄőĶ^2. For general channels we assume Vol = ‚ąę_0^1 ŌÄg^2(x;őĶ)dx = ŌÄőĶ^2, from which we have ‚ąę_0^1 h(x)dx = 1. Therefore, 1 = (‚ąę_0^1 h^{-1/2} h^{1/2})^2 ‚Č§ (‚ąę_0^1 h^{-1})(‚ąę_0^1 h) = ‚ąę_0^1 h^{-1}. The inequality indicates that the more complicated the geometry of the channel, the smaller the flux for the ion flow, which agrees with common sense.

3 Upper semi-continuity of attractors
3.1 Homogenization of boundary conditions
We convert the non-homogeneous Dirichlet boundary conditions on L‚ą™R to homogeneous ones, while keeping homogeneous Neumann conditions on M. A technical Lemma 3.1 constructs a function HőĶ with needed properties.

citation.title: Poisson-Nernst-Planck Systems for Narrow Tubular-like Membrane Channels

content:
3.2 Uniform estimates of global attractors
We derive uniform estimates of the global attractors AőĶ in őĶ. Using an inner product on L^2(ő©) and bilinear form a_őĶ, we define an operator L_őĶ. From the invariant principle and energy estimates, we obtain uniform bounds in H^1_D(ő©)√óH^1_D(ő©) for solutions and for the attractors. Key lemmas (3.2, 3.3, 3.6) provide estimates on norms and time derivatives independent of őĶ.

3.3 Upper semicontinuity
We compare solutions of the 3D problem and the 1D limiting problem. Using a projection operator M(f) (average over cross-section), we prove Lemma 3.8 that ‚ą•f-M(f)‚ą•_{H_őĶ} ‚Č§ CőĶ‚ą•f‚ą•_{X_őĶ}. Then we derive estimates for the differences P_őĶ, Q_őĶ, Ōą_őĶ (Lemma 3.9 and 3.10) that decay as őĶ‚Üí0. Finally, using the global attractor properties and the estimates, we prove Theorem 2.3.

citation.title: Poisson-Nernst-Planck Systems for Narrow Tubular-like Membrane Channels

content:
4 Steady-states for the one-dimensional limiting PNP system
Many mathematical works have been done on the existence, uniqueness and qualitative properties of boundary value problems even for high dimensional systems and algorithms have been developed toward numerical approximations (see, e.g. [15, 16, 25, 17]). Under the assumption that őľ ‚Č™ 1, the problem can be viewed as a singularly perturbed system. Typical solutions exhibit different time scales; for example, boundary and internal layers evolve at fast pace and regular layers vary slowly. For the boundary value problem (2.8)-(2.9), there are two boundary layers one at each end. Physically, near boundaries x=0 and x=1, the potential ŌÜ(x) and concentrations c1(x),c2(x) exhibit a large gradient. In [2], for őĪ1=őĪ2=1, the problem was studied using matched asymptotic expansions. In [23], geometric singular perturbation theory was applied. The treatment for the limiting one-dimensional PNP system (2.8) carrying the geometric information follows that in [23].

citation.title: Poisson-Nernst-Planck Systems for Narrow Tubular-like Membrane Channels

content:
It is convenient to study an equivalent connecting problem to the boundary value problem (2.8) and (2.9). Let B_L and B_R be subsets of R^7 defined by B_L = {ŌÜ = ŌÜ0, v = -h(0)(őĪ1l1-őĪ2l2), w = őĪ1^2 l1+őĪ2^2 l2, ŌĄ = 0}, B_R = {ŌÜ = 0, v = -h(1)(őĪ1r1-őĪ2r2), w = őĪ1^2 r1+őĪ2^2 r2, ŌĄ = 1}. The boundary value problem is equivalent to finding a solution of (2.10) from B_L to B_R.

4.1 Fast dynamics and boundary layers
System (2.14) governs the boundary layers. The slow manifold Z0 = {u=v=0} is a 5-dimensional manifold of equilibria. Each equilibrium has a 1D stable and 1D unstable manifold. System (2.14) possesses a complete set of integrals, allowing full analysis. Proposition 4.1 describes the integrals and the intersections of the stable/unstable manifolds with B_L and B_R. The boundary layers are orbits of (2.14) connecting B_L and B_R to Z0.

citation.title: Poisson-Nernst-Planck Systems for Narrow Tubular-like Membrane Channels

content:
4.2 Slow dynamics and regular layers
We examine the slow flow in the vicinity of Z0. By blowing up u = őľp, v = őľq, we obtain a singular perturbation problem. The slow manifold S0 = {p = (őĪ2J2-őĪ1J1)/w, q=0} is normally hyperbolic and persists. The limiting slow dynamics on S0 are given by a reduced system. Its solution for the regular layer connecting the two boundary layers is derived explicitly, yielding formulas for ŌÜ(x), w(x) in terms of h(x) and the constants J1,J2. Proposition 4.2 shows that the forward and backward images of ŌČ(N_L) and őĪ(N_R) intersect transversally, uniquely determining J1 and J2.

4.3 Proof of Theorem 2.4
Using the Exchange Lemma, we prove that for őľ>0 small, the manifolds Mőľ_L and Mőľ_R intersect transversally, giving a unique solution near the singular orbit. This completes the proof.

citation.title: Poisson-Nernst-Planck Systems for Narrow Tubular-like Membrane Channels

content:
4.4 A Special Case
Consider the one-dimensional limit PNP system with special boundary conditions: ŌÜ(0)=ŌÜ0, ŌÜ(1)=0, őĪ1c1(0)=őĪ1c1(1)=őĪ2c2(0)=őĪ2c2(1)=k>0. Then (c1^0, c2^0, ŌÜ^0) = (k/őĪ1, k/őĪ2, (1-x)ŌÜ0) is a steady-state. Define L(t) = ő£_{j=1}^2 (1/D_j) ‚ąę_0^1 h(x)[c_j(x,t) - c_j^0(x) ln(c_j(x,t)/c_j^0(x))] dx. Using the equation and integration by parts, L'(t) ‚Č§ 0 and L'(t)=0 iff c1=c1^0, c2=c2^0, ŌÜ=ŌÜ^0. Moreover, L(t) is equivalent to ő£ ‚ąę (c_i - c_i^0)^2 dx. Hence, ci ‚Üí ci^0 in L^2(0,1) exponentially as t‚Üí‚ąě. This shows a significant difference in asymptotic behavior between total non-flux boundary conditions and the boundary conditions considered in this work.
